XBT SOOP — measuring the ocean's temperature from the world's shipping lanes

The Ship-of-Opportunity Programme (SOOP)

The upper ocean — the top 1,000 metres — stores more than 90% of the excess heat being accumulated by Earth's climate system. How that heat is distributed, transported, and exchanged with the atmosphere is one of the most important questions in climate science. It shapes ENSO forecasts, drives regional climate patterns, and is a key indicator of long-term global warming. But measuring it across the vastness of the world's ocean basins is a formidable challenge.

Since 1980, the Ship-of-Opportunity Programme (SOOP) has been meeting that challenge by deploying oceanographic instruments from commercial vessels on regular trade routes. Rather than requiring dedicated research ships — expensive and limited in number — SOOP puts measurement systems onto vessels that are already crossing the ocean for other purposes. The result is a cost-effective, globally distributed network of ocean thermal observations that complements the Argo float programme and satellite altimetry.

What SOOP measures — the instruments

~60

active XBT ships

~19,000

XBTs deployed annually

36

repeat XBT lines

The primary instrument of SOOP is the expendable bathythermograph (XBT) — a compact temperature probe launched from the bridge wing of a ship, typically 4 to 6 times per day. The probe sinks through the water column on a thin wire, transmitting temperature at each depth to a shipboard computer that logs and formats the data. The wire breaks at the end of the probe's descent (typically around 700–900 metres), and the probe sinks to the sea floor. No recovery is required.

XBT data are automatically quality-controlled and transmitted in real time via satellite to the GTS, reaching data centres worldwide within 24 hours. In a typical year, approximately 50% of XBT deployments occur in the Pacific Ocean, 35% in the Atlantic, and 15% in the Indian Ocean. Twenty agencies are currently involved in SOOP operations.

Beyond XBTs, SOOP also coordinates thermosalinograph (TSG) deployments — sensors installed in a ship's seawater intake system that continuously record sea surface temperature and salinity as the vessel transits. TSG data provide the largest single contribution to global sea surface salinity observations from in-situ platforms. SOOP additionally supports the measurement of atmospheric and ocean carbon dioxide (pCO2), fluorescence, and pigments, and coordinates the deployment of Argo profiling floats and surface drifters from participating vessels.

Related articles

About the SOT

The Ship Observations Team coordinates global ship-based ocean and atmospheric observations — sustaining a record that has served science, safety, and climate understanding for more than 170 years.
